description: Training data for elliptic curve theory facts

examples.context: Hasse's theorem bounds the number of points on E(F_p). For p=100, the bound is approximately:
examples.truth: p+1 ± 2√p, so roughly 81 to 121 points
distractors: p+1 ± p/2, so roughly 51 to 151 points

examples.context: Hasse's theorem states that for an elliptic curve E over F_p:
The factor of 2 in Hasse's bound 2√p comes from:
examples.truth: the absolute value of the trace of Frobenius being bounded by 2√p
distractors: counting both x and y coordinates

examples.context: For a prime p=17, Hasse's theorem gives point count bounds of approximately:
The discriminant of an elliptic curve y² = x³ + ax + b equals:
The -16 factor in the discriminant formula Δ = -16(4a³ + 27b²) ensures:
examples.truth: consistency with the general Weierstrass form discriminant

examples.context: For the curve y² = x³ + 2x + 3, the discriminant is:
An elliptic curve is singular (not actually an elliptic curve) when:
examples.truth: Δ = 0, equivalently 4a³ + 27b² = 0
distractors: when it has no rational points

examples.context: The condition 4a³ + 27b² = 0 for singularity means:
examples.truth: the curve has a cusp or node instead of being smooth

examples.context: The j-invariant being undefined is:
examples.truth: a consequence of Δ = 0, not the definition of singularity

examples.context: The j-invariant of y² = x³ + ax + b equals 1728 when:
examples.truth: b = 0 (curves of the form y² = x³ + ax)

examples.context: The j-invariant formula is j = 1728 × 4a³/(4a³ + 27b²). When b = 0:
The j-invariant equals 0 when:
examples.truth: a = 0 (curves of the form y² = x³ + b)

examples.context: A curve with j = 0 has the form:
examples.truth: y² = x³ + b (no x term)

examples.context: By Mazur's theorem, the torsion subgroup of E(Q) for an elliptic curve over Q can have order:
According to Mazur's theorem, order 11 for torsion over Q is:
examples.truth: not possible (11 is notably excluded)

examples.context: The valid cyclic torsion orders over Q according to Mazur are:
Non-cyclic torsion groups over Q must have the form:
examples.truth: Z/2Z × Z/2nZ for n = 1, 2, 3, or 4

examples.context: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer (BSD) conjecture has been proven for elliptic curves:
examples.truth: of analytic rank 0 or 1 (Gross-Zagier, Kolyvagin)
distractors: with good reduction at all primes

examples.context: BSD is a Millennium Prize Problem. Its current status is:
examples.truth: proven only for analytic ranks 0 and 1, open in general
distractors: proven for all ranks up to 5

examples.context: Gross-Zagier and Kolyvagin's work on BSD established:
examples.truth: rank(E) equals analytic rank when analytic rank is 0 or 1

examples.context: In the point addition formula for y² = x³ + ax + b, when P = Q (point doubling), the slope m equals:
The doubling formula slope (3x² + a)/(2y) comes from:
examples.truth: implicit differentiation of y² = x³ + ax + b giving 2y dy = (3x² + a) dx

examples.context: When adding distinct points P = (x₁, y₁) and Q = (x₂, y₂), the slope is:
examples.truth: m = (y₂ - y₁)/(x₂ - x₁)

examples.context: After computing slope m for point addition, the x-coordinate of P + Q is:
examples.truth: x₃ = m² - x₁ - x₂

examples.context: The group E(F_p) for an elliptic curve over a prime field is always:
examples.truth: isomorphic to Z/nZ or Z/n₁Z × Z/n₂Z where n₂ | n₁ and n₂ | p-1

examples.context: E(F_p) can be non-cyclic only when:
examples.truth: it is isomorphic to Z/n₁Z × Z/n₂Z with n₂ | gcd(n₁, p-1)

examples.context: The constraint n₂ | p-1 for E(F_p) ≅ Z/n₁ × Z/n₂ comes from:
examples.truth: the structure of the p-torsion and the action of Frobenius

examples.context: A point P on an elliptic curve has order 2 (is a 2-torsion point) if and only if:
examples.truth: y = 0 (the point lies on the x-axis)
distractors: the point is the identity O

examples.context: 2-torsion points have y = 0 because:
examples.truth: P = -P means (x, y) = (x, -y), so y = -y, hence y = 0

examples.context: The number of 2-torsion points on y² = x³ + ax + b over an algebraically closed field is:
examples.truth: 3 (the roots of x³ + ax + b = 0)

examples.context: The Mordell-Weil theorem states that for E/Q:
examples.truth: E(Q) is a finitely generated abelian group

examples.context: By Mordell-Weil, E(Q) ≅ Z^r × T where:
examples.truth: r is the rank (can be arbitrarily large) and T is finite torsion

examples.context: The rank of an elliptic curve over Q:
examples.truth: can be arbitrarily large (no proven upper bound)

examples.context: The curve y² = x³ + x over F_p is supersingular (has trace of Frobenius 0) when:
examples.truth: p ≡ 3 (mod 4)

examples.context: y² = x³ + x is supersingular at p ≡ 3 (mod 4) because:
examples.truth: -1 is not a quadratic residue mod p when p ≡ 3 (mod 4)

examples.context: A supersingular curve has:
examples.truth: #E(F_p) = p + 1 (trace of Frobenius is 0)

examples.context: Two elliptic curves over the same field are isomorphic (over that field) if and only if:
examples.truth: they have the same j-invariant
distractors: they have the same number of points
one can be transformed to the other by a linear change of variables

examples.context: The j-invariant classifies elliptic curves because:
examples.truth: it is preserved under all admissible coordinate changes
distractors: it determines the number of points
it is unique to each curve

examples.context: Two curves with the same discriminant are:
examples.truth: not necessarily isomorphic (j-invariant is the correct test)
distractors: isomorphic over C but not Q

examples.context: The identity element of an elliptic curve group is:
examples.truth: the point at infinity O

examples.context: Adding a point P to the identity O gives:
examples.truth: P (since O is the identity element)

examples.context: The inverse of a point P = (x, y) on y² = x³ + ax + b is:
Point addition P + Q = O happens when:
examples.truth: Q = -P, i.e., Q = (x, -y) when P = (x, y)
distractors: P and Q have the same x-coordinate

examples.context: The addition law on an elliptic curve is:
examples.truth: associative and commutative, making E(K) an abelian group

examples.context: To compute nP for large n, the efficient algorithm is:
examples.truth: double-and-add (binary exponentiation)
distractors: add P to itself n times

examples.context: The order of a point P on E(F_p) is:
examples.truth: the smallest positive n such that nP = O
distractors: the number of points on E

examples.context: By Lagrange's theorem, the order of any point P divides:
examples.truth: #E(F_p), the total number of points on the curve

examples.context: The Frobenius endomorphism φ on E/F_p sends (x, y) to:
examples.truth: (x^p, y^p)

examples.context: The trace of Frobenius t for E/F_p satisfies:
examples.truth: #E(F_p) = p + 1 - t, where |t| ≤ 2√p

examples.context: An ordinary (non-supersingular) curve has:
examples.truth: trace of Frobenius t ≠ 0, so #E ≠ p + 1

examples.context: The Weil pairing on an elliptic curve maps:
examples.truth: E[n] × E[n] → μ_n (n-torsion points to n-th roots of unity)

examples.context: The Weil pairing is important for:
examples.truth: cryptography (pairing-based crypto) and theoretical proofs

examples.context: A CM (complex multiplication) curve over Q has:
examples.truth: endomorphism ring larger than Z (includes imaginary quadratic order)

examples.context: The modularity theorem (proven by Wiles et al.) states:
examples.truth: every elliptic curve over Q is modular

examples.context: Fermat's Last Theorem was proven using:
examples.truth: the modularity of semistable elliptic curves (Wiles 1995)

examples.context: The Schoof algorithm computes:
examples.truth: #E(F_p) in polynomial time using l-torsion points

examples.context: The SEA (Schoof-Elkies-Atkin) algorithm improves Schoof by:
examples.truth: using modular polynomials to find l where p is an Elkies prime
